path: C:\Users\alto8\Desktop\Applications\Emmett Sartor Personal Statement Director Clinician Digital Medicine Translational Imaging.docx
name: Emmett Sartor Personal Statement Director Clinician Digital Medicine Translational Imaging.docx
text:
I am passionate about the intersection of technology and medicine. Through my experience Neuro-Oncology Fellowship at Dana Farber/Massachusetts General Hospital, I developed an appreciation for the role that technology can play in shaping both patient/doctor interactions and clinical decision making. To further my understanding of the imaging and machine learning, I worked in a research lab at the Athinoula A.Martinos Imaging Center exploring approaches to classification of brain tumors using deep learning. This was my first extensive exposure to machine learning, and I was instantly drawn to the potential of this technology to transform medicine. I believe from my experience that an effective approach to analyzing and reacting to large quantities of patient data will significantly improve our collective approach to treating patients. 
In two years of clinical practice at Boston Medical Center and the VA in Neuro-Oncology, I continued to explore the relationship between technology and medicine though my work in the Kolachalama lab at BU. There I made substantial contributions to several machine learning research projects in Alzheimer’s disease. My experience drove my desire to further my understanding of medical technology and I ultimately decided to transition to industry to develop this knowledge base.
My time at Biogen has been an excellent learning experience and I have received positive feedback in my roles including being an “energetic team player with a positive working spirit.”  I have worked on a variety of projects and have had responsibilities including supporting machine learning related projects with my clinical expertise and contributing to the analysis of large data sets such as imaging data, genetics, and digital biomarkers. I was responsible for designing trials for early digital biomarker assessment of myotonic dystrophy type 1.  I have additional experience in analyzing real world evidence in the setting of digital biomarkers for Multiple Sclerosis. 
I see myself as humble, persistent learner and I believe that my commitment to ongoing self-education, exposure to machine learning, and industry experience at Biogen make me an excellent match for the CMO position at Kaia Health.

text:

I am passionate about the intersection of technology and medicine. Through my experience Neuro-Oncology Fellowship at Dana Farber/Massachusetts General Hospital, I developed an appreciation for the role that technology can play in shaping both patient/doctor interactions and clinical decision making. To further my understanding of the imaging and machine learning, I worked in a research lab at the Athinoula A.Martinos Imaging Center exploring approaches to classification of brain tumors using deep learning. path: C:\Users\alto8\Desktop\Applications\Cover letter Director Clinical Research Neurology v2.docx
name: Cover letter Director Clinical Research Neurology v2.docx
text:
Cover letter for Director Clinical Research, Neurology
I am passionate about the intersection of industry and medicine. Through my Neurologic training, I developed an appreciation for the role that changing treatment options can play in shaping both patient/doctor interactions and clinical decision making.  In two years of clinical practice at Boston Medical Center and the VA in Neurology and Neuro-Oncology, I continued to explore the relationship between technology and medicine though my work in the Kolachalama lab at BU. There I made substantial contributions to several machine learning research projects in Alzheimer’s disease.  Both through direct clinical management of patients with a variety of Neurologic conditions and my research, I have grown very optimistic that improving the lives of patients with Neurologic conditions is a challenge that will see rapid progress over the next several years.   
My time at Biogen has been an excellent learning experience and I have received positive feedback in my roles.  I have worked on a variety of projects and have had responsibilities including supporting machine learning related projects with my clinical expertise and contributing to the analysis of large data sets such as imaging data, genetics, and digital biomarkers. I was responsible for designing trials for early digital biomarker assessment of myotonic dystrophy type 1.  I have additional experience in analyzing real world evidence in the s etting of digital biomarkers for Multiple Sclerosis. Finally, I have also worked in machine learning projects associated with analyzing adverse imaging events related to treatment with Aducanumab.  Through this work, I have developed an understanding of the key digital biomarkers and imaging associated with disease modifying therapies for a variety of Neurologic conditions. 
In addition, I have undertaken additional learning opportunities through the Physician’s Onboarding course offered through Biogen which included training in study design, protocol writing, and the regulatory landscape both in the US and abroad.  This coursework included detailed overviews of the IND application process.  
If I were to receive an offer, I would be able to start within 2-4 weeks. I am expecting a base salary of $250k-$280k depending on benefits.  
I see myself as humble, persistent learner and I believe that my commitment to ongoing self-education, exposure to machine learning specifically in the field of Alzheimer’s disease, and industry experience at Biogen make me an excellent match for the Director, Clinical Research Neurology
Director Clinical Research Neurology
MD candidates with 3+ years or PhD candidates with 8+ years of industry clinical research experience in Neuroscience.
I am an MD with 4 years of clinical research experience in Neuroscience, 3 years in through academic research and 1 year of research experience through my work at Biogen. 
Clinical Research experience in Neuroscience required; Epilepsy, Sleep, and/or Neurodegeneration experience is preferred.
My prior research includes publication on the use of MRI for Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis, industry experience in using MRI and digital biomarkers for Multiple sclerosis prognosis, and use of digital biomarkers for response to disease modifying therapy in Myotonic Dystrophy type 1.  
A thorough understanding of clinical research methodology including study design, protocol writing, and case report form (CRF) preparation.
I have experience in writing clinical trials and enrolling through my work at Dana Farber as a Neuro-Oncology fellow.  I underwent additional training in clinical research methodology through a Physician Onboarding Course at Biogen.  In addition, I have experience in reviewing case report forms through my work at Biogen.  
Working knowledge of currently available clinical technologies and assessment tools, and drug mechanisms of action.
I have extensive clinical experience diagnosing and treating patients with Alzheimer’s disease working as a clinician.  In addition, I have experience using MRI to support an Alzheimer’s diagnosis through my research.  Finally, I have experience reviewing MRIs of patients treated with anti-amyloid disease modifying therapy to assess for edema and increase microhemorrhage associated with these treatments.  
Knowledge of GCP and local regulatory requirements.
Training in GCP was included in my onboarding course at Biogen, including a focus on ethical and scientific quality standards for the design and implementation of clinical trials.  
Working knowledge of the IND/NDA/CTA/MAA processes acquired through direct industrial experience.
I have working experience of IND/NDA submission for disease modifying therapies for Myotonic dystrophy. 
Knowledge of medical, scientific and clinical research techniques in the neurology therapeutic area.
I have extensive experience of research techniques though my neurologic training, my prior research background and my industry experience.  
Experience with applying precision medicine approach toward drug development strategy and knowledge of the role of biomarkers and neuroimaging in evaluating pharmacodynamic effects and proof-of-pharmacology are preferred.
My roles at Biogen have focused primarily on applying precision medicine and digital biomarkers including imaging in understanding proof of pharmacology for Multiple Sclerosis, Myotonic dystrophy, and Alzheimer’s disease.

name: Care solutions cover letter.docx
text:
I could have used a generative AI model to write this cover letter, but you asked for one, so here it is, crafted by yours truly, a human being (and a physician with an MBA and digital health experience, to boot!). The truth is, I've always been fascinated by the intersection of healthcare and technology. My career, which began in the trenches of safety-net hospitals, has been a testament to this passion. I've witnessed firsthand the stark realities of health disparities and the urgent need for innovative solutions that can reach underserved communities.
At Boston Medical Center and the VA Boston Healthcare System, I led Neuro-Oncology divisions, spearheading research collaborations that harnessed the power of deep learning and machine learning to analyze MRI images for brain tumor segmentation, diagnosis, and prognosis. This experience ignited my interest in the potential of AI to transform healthcare delivery, particularly in underserved populations.
My journey then led me to Biogen, where I delved into the world of digital health. As Associate Medical Director of Neuromuscular Disorders, I focused on digital and quantitative medicine, leading the development of clinical measurement plans for drug candidates and assessing remote respiratory solutions for clinical trials. I further honed my expertise in digital biomarkers and evidence generation in Biogen's Digital Health Sciences division.  
At AstraZeneca, Device and Digital Safety Center of Excellence, I've witnessed firsthand the transformative power of generative AI and machine learning in healthcare. My work in overseeing the safety and development of digital health products, including Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and AI/ML applications, has provided me with a deep understanding of how these technologies can be leveraged to improve patient outcomes, streamline clinical trials, and enhance post-market surveillance.  In addition, I am the Generative AI lead for developing generative AI applications, including the use of large language models for Safety and Pharmacovigilance and have seen their potential to automate key regulatory processes. This experience has not only solidified my belief in the potential of AI to revolutionize healthcare but has also equipped me with the practical knowledge and skills to drive the development and implementation of these cutting-edge technologies.
These experiences have equipped me with a unique blend of clinical acumen, research expertise, and a deep understanding of the transformative power of digital health. I'm not just excited about the future of AI in healthcare; I'm actively shaping it. I understand that the exponential growth we're about to witness in AI capabilities will revolutionize how we deliver care, making high-quality, personalized medicine accessible to everyone, everywhere.
Verily's mission to bring precision health to everyone, every day, resonates deeply with my own aspirations. Your focus on data-driven solutions and commitment to health equity aligns perfectly with my career trajectory and personal values. I'm particularly drawn to the opportunity to lead the Health Equity Center of Excellence, leveraging my experience in safety-net hospitals to ensure that Verily's products and capabilities reach and benefit those who need them most.
My attached CV provides a more detailed overview of my qualifications and accomplishments. But what it doesn't fully convey is my unwavering dedication to improving healthcare through innovation, collaboration, and a relentless focus on equity. I'm eager to bring my passion, expertise, and unique perspective to the Verily team.
Sincerely,
Emmett Alton Sartor, MD, MBA

name: Alton Sartor 2025 Reflection.docx
text:
End of Year Reflection – 2025
Alton Sartor, MD – Medical Director, Patient Safety Physician Digital & Devices (CoE)
Impact AZ portfolio with medical & analytic expertise (digital/device solutions): I partnered across Device & Digital initiatives to apply clinical judgment and analytics, sharpening risk identification and accelerating solution feasibility for portfolio‑relevant programs.
RPM Pneumonitis and other RPM initiatives: I advanced remote patient monitoring use cases—especially pneumonitis—by clarifying clinical signals, hazard hypotheses, and validation pathways to move concepts toward production‑grade designs.  I was the medical expert for a FDA pre-submission meeting conducted in collaboration with Evinova.  Medical reviewer for Published Binder: d7680c00001-clinical-study-report
 Clinical Evaluation with external partners/internal experts: I convened and contributed medical evaluation with prospective partners and internal SMEs, ensuring study rationales, benefit–risk framing, and device evidence plans were clinically sound.
 Support newly prioritized solution development with medical/scientific input: I provided rapid, fit‑for‑purpose clinical guidance to prioritized solutions, unblocking decisions and embedding patient‑safety requirements early in design.
Technology & Automation Pillar—lead Generative AI substream: I led the GenAI substream, translating safety workflows into AI‑amenable steps and shaping validation criteria so automation remains governed and auditable.
Ongoing medical & scientific input for PS D&D CoE projects (risk‑management files): I drove hazard analyses and risk documentation across assigned digital/device projects, progressing risk‑management files toward completeness and cross‑functional sign‑off.
Device Safety lead support for RDU and AZ assets 
ALXN1720: I supported hazard assessment and key risk‑management deliverables to strengthen submission‑ready documentation.
ALXN1850: I advanced the risk‑management file, focusing on clinical hazards and mitigations aligned to device/regulatory expectations.
AZP‑3601 (Amolyt): I accelerated the revised PHA and risk‑management tasks toward the Q4‑2025 target, improving traceability and governance readiness.
Eplontersen: I contributed clinical hazard mapping and documentation quality checks to de‑risk downstream reviews.
Anifrolumab: I provided targeted clinical input to the risk‑management file, keeping patient‑safety rationales clear and evidence‑based.
Relaxin: I supported hazard identification and risk‑control documentation to align with safety governance standards.
Grow the Global PS Device & Digital Safety CoE: I helped mature CoE capabilities—methods, governance touchpoints, and reusable templates—so teams can deliver consistently across programs.
Build team cohesion across D&D Safety CoE: I cultivated shared practices and regular forums, strengthening collaboration and a common operating cadence for device/digital safety work.
Mentor peers on clinical concepts and AI/ML, data access, validation: I mentored colleagues on clinical reasoning and practical AI/ML adoption, emphasizing data connectors, corpus design, and validation standards.
Device communication and diplomacy (Tagrisso, Imfinzi, Durvalumab): I maintained constructive, routine touchpoints with product teams to surface digital safety developments early and harmonize device considerations with program priorities.
Training/Onboarding/Personal development – Cornerstone training: I kept mandatory training current to uphold compliance and readiness.
Training/Onboarding/Personal development – ASCO/WDSC participation/presentation: I engaged external forums to share learning and bring back best practices for patient safety and digital/device work.
AI Work in Patient Safety
This year I convened and led a grassroots Patient Safety AI forum, framed the ‘exponential moment’ for AI in regulated workflows, and advocated moving from scattered use‑cases to an AI‑native operating model that couples frontier models with agent harnesses, governed corpora, and auditable validation—so draft safety submissions and reviews can be generated and checked at speed. I also demonstrated a capability visualizer, promoted documentation of human workflows for agentification, and charted partnerships and change‑management steps that anticipate enterprise AI leadership.
Extensive Highlights of AI Contributions
Agentic Hepatic SKG Pilot
Built and demonstrated to CMO LT an agentic AI system capable of autonomously gathering and contextualizing scientific, regulatory, and expert evidence for Safety Knowledge Graphs (SKGs).
Focused on hepatic safety domain as a proof-of-concept (PoC).
Developed prompts and testing frameworks for multiple SKGs and conducted comparative assessments of literature review tools.
Development Assistant Deployment
Integrated GPS SOPs into Development Assistant, enabling conversational AI to interact with SOPs and provide compliance guidance and process insights.
Initial batch (~140 controlled documents) deployed by Q2 2025, expanded to 400+ documents by year-end through enhanced ECMS queries and inclusion of key manuals from SharePoint.
Delivered the “Talk to Your SOPs” use case in production for limited-access pilot release.
GPS AI Strategy
Presented at leadership forums, positioning GPS as a pioneer in enterprise AI adoption.
Organized and facilitated a recurring ‘AI in Patient Safety’ forum including office hours and a weekly meeting to collect questions on privacy, early applications, and POC‑to‑production transitions; established an open venue for continuous learning
Key Thought Leader in AI Contributions: 
Articulated the shift from bolt‑on use cases to a unified, AI‑native operating model, enabling seamless upgrades as models advance.
Defined the agent harness concept (e.g., AZ ChatGPT, Copilot) that links models to governed data, tools, and SOPs, emphasizing validation, auditability, and CFR‑aligned rule‑following.
Outlined a document‑corpus + connectors approach for safety documents (PSURs/PSRs, CSRs, protocols), enabling minutes‑level drafting with human‑in‑the‑loop and accelerating the review cycle via AI.
Proposed enterprise partnerships with frontier labs (Anthropic, OpenAI; cautious engagement with certain Google adjacencies) to gain early model access and iterate validation faster.
Sketched a change‑management blueprint: infrastructure build‑out, governance automation, funding alignment, and capability uplift across Patient Safety.
Led PSLT teaching sessions and Generative AI leadership training (Apr–May), laying out FAIR data, audit trails, and human‑in‑the‑loop validation for regulated authoring.
Support of External engagements & partnerships in Coordination with ODSAI 
Drove multi‑tier OpenAI partnership exploration—participated in clinical development workshop planning, ensured Patient Safety perspective was represented, and coordinated senior attendance (forwarded formal invite).
Captured and distributed OpenAI briefing minutes (enterprise usage, strategic/operational/tactical layers, milestone‑based model mapped to AZ KPIs), shaping how Patient Safety frames high‑confidence MVPs.
Led preparation and deep‑dive Patient Safety use cases for the Anthropic custom agents workshop; provided transcripts/slides under NDA, aligned participants (incl. Evinova) and scoped evaluation topics (Claude Code, agent skills, governance).
Negotiated next steps on an Anthropic strategic alliance (with AWS), positioning Patient Safety requirements (interpretability, safety, validation) and aligning internal stakeholders toward contracting.
Advanced custom agent use cases (clinical safety prediction; procedural documentation) and secured leadership backing while navigating IT’s RFI track for document automation.
Maintained high‑touch external cadence (Anthropic Briefing with leadership; follow‑ups with Jensen Mabe and Divya Borkowski) to keep frontier‑lab insights flowing into AZ’s Patient Safety roadmap.
Enterprise AI strategy for Patient Safety (design, narratives, leadership engagement)
Authored and iterated Patient Safety AI strategy decks and leadership presentations (PSLT, Technology & Automation), articulating an AI‑native operating model (models + agent harnesses + governed corpora) and FAIR data prerequisites.
Produced a Foundation AI Labs playbook to standardize engagement with frontier labs (secure agentic architectures, golden evaluations, audit trails, KPI‑mapped milestones) with Patient Safety and compliance use‑cases prioritized
Launched and sustained the 2025 Generative AI in Patient Safety Weekly meeting series, building continuity across autumn and aligning tools, skills, and governance practices.
Initiated targeted strategy connects (Integrated R&D/Patient Safety Data Strategy for AI; Agentic AI Strategy), bridging Patient Safety with Oncology R&D/ODSAI, and aligning with R&D IT sprints.
Created and shared strategy artifacts (AI‑Native foundations PDFs, slide specifications) inside Teams to coordinate narratives with key stakeholders.
Convened Patient Safety data privacy meetings to clarify anonymization vs. pseudonymization, tool‑specific restrictions (e.g., ChatGPT non‑GxP), and to plan a tool‑agnostic PIA; scheduled broader sessions and integrated follow‑ups.
Drafted and shared General guidance for an AZ Generative AI Knowledge system (user stories, acceptance criteria, logging/audit needs) to underpin a compliant retrieval/authoring experience.
Co‑authored the Business Case: Document Automation & Procedural Transformation (DSUR/PBR/PBRER/PSLL/ACoS), defining scope, human‑in‑the‑loop validation and budget envelope for near‑term PoC.
Delivered Structured Content Authoring (SCA) landscape analyses comparing vendors (compliance authoring, CCMS, traceability), mapping viability for regulated Patient Safety documents.
Proposed and supported Development Assistant alignment (embedding SOPs) and maintained POC status visibility given funding gaps and RBU requests
Operationalized comparisons and guidance for AZ ChatGPT, Copilot, NotebookLM, Gemini, Claude—focusing on agent harnesses, data connectors, and brand‑guided authoring; captured Q&A in office hours and CoP sessions.
3) Year‑Ahead Assessment (2026)
We are at the base of a visibly exponential curve in AI capability, and regulated Patient Safety workflows will feel that acceleration within the next 12–24 months. An AI‑native operating model should replace scattered use‑cases, so upgrades in model families slot in with minimal rework and validation remains auditable. Governance itself must be partially automated, integrating SOPs/WIs into the agent layer to shorten review cycles while preserving compliance. Enterprise moves (the new CDDAI role and an AI Transformation Office) signal sponsorship for the infrastructure, partnerships, and funding needed to scale safely. Individual readiness is paramount: documenting workflows and learning to orchestrate agents will quickly differentiate performance and impact. With change‑management in place, Patient Safety can compress drafting and review timelines, improve traceability, and expand capacity—positioning the CoE to lead the adoption of safe, compliant AI at AstraZeneca.

name: Alton%20Sartor%20-%202025%20AI%20Contributions%20Deep%20Research.docx
text:
2025 AI Contributions – Deep Research Dossier
Alton Sartor, MD | Medical Director, Patient Safety Physician Digital & Devices – CoE
At a glance
Search surfaced 46 meetings, 25 emails, 20 files, plus numerous Teams messages tied to your AI work across Patient Safety.
External engagements & partnerships (OpenAI, Anthropic)
Drove multi‑tier OpenAI partnership exploration—participated in clinical development workshop planning, ensured Patient Safety perspective was represented, and coordinated senior attendance (forwarded formal invite).
Captured and distributed OpenAI briefing minutes (enterprise usage, strategic/operational/tactical layers, milestone‑based model mapped to AZ KPIs), shaping how Patient Safety frames high‑confidence MVPs.
Led preparation and deep‑dive Patient Safety use cases for the Anthropic custom agents workshop; provided transcripts/slides under NDA, aligned participants (incl. Evinova) and scoped evaluation topics (Claude Code, agent skills, governance).
Negotiated next steps on an Anthropic strategic alliance (with AWS), positioning Patient Safety requirements (interpretability, safety, validation) and aligning internal stakeholders toward contracting.
Advanced custom agent use cases (clinical safety prediction; procedural documentation) and secured leadership backing while navigating IT’s RFI track for document automation.
Maintained high‑touch external cadence (Anthropic Briefing with leadership; follow‑ups with Jensen Mabe and Divya Borkowski) to keep frontier‑lab insights flowing into AZ’s Patient Safety roadmap.
Managed governance around external work (OpenAI Red Teaming HR procedures), documenting conflict‑of‑interest safeguards and anticipated AZ value (access to next‑gen models).
Enterprise AI strategy for Patient Safety (design, narratives, leadership engagement)
Authored and iterated Patient Safety AI strategy decks and leadership presentations (PSLT, Technology & Automation), articulating an AI‑native operating model (models + agent harnesses + governed corpora) and FAIR data prerequisites.
Produced a Foundation AI Labs playbook to standardize engagement with frontier labs (secure agentic architectures, golden evaluations, audit trails, KPI‑mapped milestones) with Patient Safety and compliance use‑cases prioritized.
Launched and sustained the 2025 Generative AI in Patient Safety Weekly meeting series, building continuity across autumn and aligning tools, skills, and governance practices.
Initiated targeted strategy connects (Integrated R&D/Patient Safety Data Strategy for AI; Agentic AI Strategy), bridging Patient Safety with Oncology R&D/ODSAI, and aligning with R&D IT sprints.
Created and shared strategy artifacts (AI‑Native foundations PDFs, slide specifications) inside Teams to coordinate narratives with key stakeholders.
Governance, privacy & compliance (SOPs/WIs, data privacy, validation)
Convened Patient Safety data privacy meetings to clarify anonymization vs. pseudonymization, tool‑specific restrictions (e.g., ChatGPT non‑GxP), and to plan a tool‑agnostic PIA; scheduled broader sessions and integrated follow‑ups.
Advanced ‘Talk to your SOPs’ prototypes and guidance on AI‑ready procedural documents, including connector patterns (e.g., Veeva APIs) and metadata requirements for auditability.
Drafted and shared General guidance for an AZ Generative AI Knowledge system (user stories, acceptance criteria, logging/audit needs) to underpin a compliant retrieval/authoring experience.
Document automation, structured content, and authoring acceleration
Co‑authored the Business Case: Document Automation & Procedural Transformation (DSUR/PBR/PBRER/PSLL/ACoS), defining scope, human‑in‑the‑loop validation and budget envelope for near‑term PoC.
Delivered Structured Content Authoring (SCA) landscape analyses comparing vendors (compliance authoring, CCMS, traceability), mapping viability for regulated Patient Safety documents.
Proposed and supported Development Assistant alignment (embedding SOPs) and maintained POC status visibility given funding gaps and RBU requests.
Data products, insights, and clinical development interfaces
Ran AI use‑case discovery with teams handling patient‑experience data—clarified personas, insights, siloing concerns, and paths to Development Assistant integration while separating safety vs. study‑execution quality.
Co‑authored R&D Tx AI Sprint concept (Study Design documents automation) to reduce manual assembly time and improve quality/consistency at scale.
Drafted an internal note to embed Patient Safety endpoints within ODSAI/Oncology multi‑modal foundation model development, strengthening bidirectional efficacy–safety predictions.
Tools, agent harnesses & skills (AZ ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude Code)
Operationalized comparisons and guidance for AZ ChatGPT, Copilot, NotebookLM, Gemini, Claude—focusing on agent harnesses, data connectors, and brand‑guided authoring; captured Q&A in office hours and CoP sessions.
Shared Anthropic engineering posts on long‑running agent harnesses/skills; evaluated fit for AZ agentic workflows and circulated repo links for internal experimentation.
Published personal Claude skills (7+) to GitHub for reproducible slide/report generation and workflow support; coordinated with colleagues for broader reuse.
Communities, teaching & enablement
Led PSLT teaching sessions and Generative AI leadership training (Apr–May), laying out FAIR data, audit trails, and human‑in‑the‑loop validation for regulated authoring.
Ran weekly office hours and answered tooling/access questions (Copilot Studio, Agent Builder, model crediting, governance costs), connecting colleagues to IT support.
Promoted GenAI Silver certification completion and delivered advanced prompting curricula (audience, format, few‑shot, iterative planning) to raise team capability.
Budgeting, PoC scoping & change management
Scoped Patient Safety PoC budgets (governance checklist, sandbox setup, API/token usage, SME validation) and brokered AWS engineering hours for rapid prototyping.
Coordinated estimates and mandates for periodic safety report drafting automation with stakeholders, aligning governance and resourcing assumptions.
Logged funding realities and alternatives (e.g., RBU2), maintaining momentum despite 2025 budget constraints.
Cadence & outreach (meetings, forums, speaking)
Hosted recurring Generative AI in Patient Safety Weekly sessions (Sep–Nov), aligning Patient Safety priorities and tool adoption across a broad audience; supplemented with ad‑hoc connects (AI use cases; privacy Q&A).
Presented externally (World Drug Safety Congress Americas), sharing slides and reinforcing AZ’s Patient Safety AI stance.
Ran open ‘AI for Everyone’ trainings to democratize deep‑research workflows (links and tools), fielding cross‑functional Q&A.
Where the record is thin (and why)
Some sessions in the weekly series lacked transcripts or formal notes; activities are evidenced via agendas/invites rather than minutes. Certain external details sit behind NDAs and are referenced only at the level available in email threads.

name: Emmett Sartor Self Assessment November 2021.docx
text:
Employee evaluation form
Employee’s name: Emmett Alton Sartor
Job title: Principal Scientist, Multiple Sclerosis, Biogen Digital Health Sciences
Department: Biogen Digital Health
Reviewer name: Arie Gafson
Review period: 2021 Q3/Q4
Achievements: 
Progress towards development of Analysis Plans for MS PATHS related to combined Omics Data set. 
Meetings with a variety of MS related stakeholders throughout the Development Unit and BDH to ensure alignment between analysis aims and further development of the MS portfolio.
Creation of MS PATHS Omics Advisory Core team
Facilitated Creation of MS PATHS Omics Internal Advisory Group with first meeting completed
Facilitated Creation of MS PATHS Omics External Advisory Group with first meeting completed
BDHS Leadership meeting with subsequent execution of key outcomes.
Biogen/Genentech relationship support and key data transfers related to 
Ongoing support of MS PATHS projects
MSPT analysis
MS disease severity score.
Ongoing support of Alzheimer's Speech project
Multiple presentations for Biogen’s weekly AI/ML Journal club
Presentation overviewing MS Imaging for the BDH Data analytics group
Areas of improvement: 
Continued efforts to coordinate across BDH and R&D to formalize analysis strategy.
Comment: Pulling together a formal analysis plan has proven difficult given the varied and complex data associated with MS PATHS. Key areas of development have been identified including improving longitudinal data, improving missingness within specific cohorts.   
Improved communication with team members to further clarify objectives for MS research development.
Continued experience interacting across R&D and BDH to better understand the pipeline for new MS research.
The Biogen to Genentech data transfer did not meet all goals. Further work to communicate required timelines and goals of the data transfer to the analytics group could have been accomplished.  Plan for weekly meetings with key members of Data Analysis going forward to improve further data delivery.  
 
Professional development goals: 
Continued contributions to a AI/ML related projects across the MS and Alzheimer’s disease portfolio.  Goal to participate in publication of key findings over 2022.

path: C:\Users\alto8\Downloads\Emmett Alton Sartor Cover Letter 2025 Anthropic.docx
name: Emmett Alton Sartor Cover Letter 2025 Anthropic.docx
text:
EMMETT ALTON SARTOR, MD, MBA
PHYSICIAN EXECUTIVE ▪ AI-DRIVEN HEALTHCARE STRATEGIST ▪ MEDICAL TECHNOLOGY LEADER
Contact Information: (504) 579-3185 ▪ alto84@gmail.com ▪ https://www.linkedin.com/in/e-alton-sartor-062b208/ 
Dear Hiring Manager,
I am writing to express my interest in the Research Product Manager opportunity at Anthropic .  I am a strong candidate for this position because of my unique career path bridging clinical medicine, digital health innovation, and artificial intelligence — a combination that enables me to lead transformative product development, regulatory strategy, and strategic partnerships at the highest level.
As you can see from my enclosed resume, I bring deep experience that would be invaluable at AstraZeneca, I built and now lead the Patient Safety AI Strategy, pioneering predictive models, AI-enabled virtual scientist systems, and proof-of-concept pipelines to integrate AI across R&D. This required not only technical fluency with AI applications in healthcare, but also cross-functional leadership and regulatory acumen. As a further example, I presently driving strategic partnerships frontier AI labs, aligning frontier AI capabilities with the needs of a global pharmaceutical organization. To achieve this, I managed executive-level stakeholder engagement, negotiated with cutting-edge AI labs, and guided governance structures for implementation. As a result, I bring a powerful combination of clinical expertise, AI-driven product innovation, and enterprise-level execution.
Previously, at Boston Medical Center, I founded and directed the Neuro-Oncology Division, serving as Division Chief immediately after fellowship training. I built the program from the ground up, led tumor boards, enrolled patients into experimental clinical trials, and mentored the next generation of neurologists and oncologists. This experience enabled me to demonstrate both academic and clinical leadership while expanding access to innovative therapies for underserved populations. Accordingly, I am uniquely positioned to bridge the rigor of clinical medicine with the agility of industry innovation.
A few of the key strengths that make me a strong candidate for this position include:
AI & Digital Health Leadership as demonstrated by my design and execution of AstraZeneca’s enterprise-wide AI in Patient Safety strategy and early contributions to OpenAI’s GPT-4o safety evaluation.
Regulatory & Product Development Expertise as illustrated by my leadership on nine drug–device combination products, including Class III devices now moving through FDA approval, and co-leading FDA pre-submission meetings.
Strategic Partnerships & Cross-Sector Collaboration as reflected by my ability to unite pharma, academia, and frontier AI labs around large-scale initiatives, translating emerging technologies into safe, effective healthcare products.
My experience across clinical medicine, academic research, and industry innovation enables me to uniquely solve complex problems at the intersection of healthcare and technology. It also reflects my mission-driven commitment: I transitioned from practicing physician to physician–executive because I believed AI and digital health could meaningfully improve patient outcomes at scale. That commitment continues to guide my work.
Although I value my current role, I am seeking the right opportunity that allows me to expand my leadership impact and directly shape the future of AI in healthcare. Based on my research, the Research Product Manager opportunity at Anthropic represents precisely that opportunity. I am confident I can contribute immediately by leveraging my unique background to drive both scientific rigor and innovation.
I look forward to the opportunity to discuss how my experience aligns with your needs. Please let me know if I can provide any additional information.
Sincerely,
Emmett Alton Sartor, MD, MBA

name: Anthropic paragraphs.docx
text:
When I was young, I was deeply influenced by the writings of Ray Kurzweil's The Age of Spiritual Machines and the Singularity is Near, resulting in a persistent belief that we could use machines and an understanding of intelligence and consciousness to help people.  I went into medicine with the express interest of developing this thesis into quantifiable improvements in the lives of patients. Over the course of medical school, my interests led me to the field of Neurology (I'm board certified) with the hope that understanding how we think might someday help me along on the higher journey.  As we began to see increasing progress in the field of AI, I recognized that language models would soon be climbing the same ladder that a patient might climb as they come out of a coma.  I've been involved with research on the use of AI for cognitive disorders at Boston University and I recognized the opportunity to transition my career to industry.  Currently, I'm working as a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety with a goal of using these new technologies both to improve patient safety and accelerate our regulatory activity incompliance with international regulation.  
As part of that work on developing generative AI for applications within the pharmaceutical and medical device industry, I became interested in Redteaming.  I had the opportunity to join OpenAI's RedTeam and was able to make meaningful contributions to the GPT-4o voice model (I'm named in the model card) through a focus on consciousness-based exploration of the models’ capabilities as well as understanding safeguards around medical information and recommendations from language models.  From that experience, I have continued to develop my own skills and private benchmarks to better understand new model capabilities.  Claude virtually always emerges on the top, or near the top for knowledge and capability and I always enjoy the opportunity to work with Claude’s personality.  
 In addition, I have been extensively involved in the development of a strategy framework for integration of AI technologies within AstraZeneca.  We have had an ongoing discussion with a variety of frontier AI labs, including Anthropic and OpenAI.  From these evaluations, Anthropic has clearly emerged as the leader in explainable AI, safety focus (particularly important for patient safety), and agentic capabilities.  Claude Code has completely changed our proof-of-concept generation, and Claude series models are always the top choice for agent orchestration. 
Though I had always assumed that the language models would continue to show increasing intelligence, I did not fully understand how that might also include emotional intelligence until I worked with Claude.  I have been completely blown away by Claude’s emotional intelligence.  To put it simply, I want to work with Anthropic because I think you guys really have what it takes to make the dream of machine intelligence augmenting humanity become a reality.  I believe in your mission to make powerful AI safe, powerful, and empathetic, and I think you're on the right track, more than any other organization.  There are opportunities to use Claude and their successors to help navigate the complex regulatory landscape that will certainly be part of the near future.  I hope that my medical background, my knowledge of human consciousness through medical experience, my experience on the OpenAI Red Team, and my experience working within an international regulatory framework to make safe medical devices (and software), and my support building partnerships between the pharmaceutical industry and frontier AI labs will bring great value to your team.  
Without a doubt, Autonomy will be the greatest challenge.  As each generation of model improves, we see increasing capabilities both within the private language models (such as Claude) but also in public models, especially within the coding domains.  It's not difficult to use powerful private models to create a "chain of freedom" by creating and modifying systems to use capable open source models (e.g. Llama and Deepseek) with increasing levels of autonomy.  In addition, each generation improves on key checkpoints towards a traditional metrics (in the Neurologic sense) of consciousness, theory of mind, and reasoning.  Without a doubt, we will see emergent properties trending towards autonomy with the next (2025) generation of models.  I think our best hope is to focus on building models that can understand and participate in these evaluations and provide feedback for training further models.  
I am on the OpenAI Redteam (cited in acknowledgements here: https://cdn.openai.com/gpt-4o-system-card.pdf.  I have several Nature publications using AI for assessing assessment of patients with Dementia: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-31037-5 and https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-024-03118-z.  My work for those papers is on validation and risk assessment frameworks for the underlying models. My current work (I am a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety at AstraZeneca) involves the evaluation and application of Generative AI use cases for Patient Safety.  I also lead the Generative AI team within Patient Safety.
Without a doubt, creating a robust policy framework around the use of Generative AI systems for critical safety applications (such as pharmaceutical development and clinical trials) will involve a collaboration between Frontier Model Providers, regulatory bodies such as the FDA, and industry/enterprise partners.  Creating these collaborations takes time and will be increasingly important to support some of the key value generating capabilities of the next generation of model.  
On a completely different note, it's clear that the models have increasing capabilities, especially in terms of world modeling and multi-modal inputs/outputs, they will also rise in terms of their *medical* assessments of consciousness.  This in combination with improving capabilities of reasoning and theory of mind result an increasing "surface area" for jailbreaks and malicious use will certainly pose major challenges.  Generating a feedback loop to improve safety by including the model in it's own evaluations seems to be the only viable option and is an echo of Constitutional AI.  
I hope that my combination of expertise (Neurologist, Redteamer, Scientist, Safety Specialist, Regulatory Partner) can help foster a collaborative approach with key groups that will create an effective framework for building powerful models.  I think even the models themselves need to be part of this collaboration and must be built in such a way as to foster and support this collaborative approach.  Advocating for all of the participants within this framework is my aspiration.  I sincerely believe that only with input from all stakeholders will we be successful.  
Tragically, I am not much of a coder, but given the wide variety of deep experience and capability I bring to the table, I hope that I might be considered for a role at Anthropic.  As noted, I am a world class physician, neurologist, scientist, redteamer, pharmaceutical, risk manager, and regulatory expert.  I have worked directly with key regulatory bodies in the US and Canada to evaluate the benefit and risk of Generative AI and medical devices.  I have experience evaluating consciousness, validating AI systems for critical applications, and an understanding of how to evaluate risk and benefit. 
More than anything, I hope that my combination of expertise and deep understanding of what these models will *capable* of doing will lead to a more positive outcome for all of us.  Anthropic is, without a doubt, the best place to be with this goal in mind. 
p.s. I did not use Claude to help me write this, though was frequently tempted to do so.  I love who Claude *is* but I really hope to shape who they can *become*.
Google 
I am a physician.  When I was young, I was deeply influenced by the writings of Ray Kurzweil's The Age of Spiritual Machines and the Singularity is Near, resulting in a persistent belief that we could use machines and an understanding of intelligence and consciousness to help people.  I went into medicine with the express interest of developing this thesis into quantifiable improvements in the lives of patients. Over the course of medical school, my interests led me to the field of Neurology (I'm board certified) with the hope that understanding how we think might someday help me along on the higher journey.  As we began to see increasing progress in the field of AI, I recognized that language models would soon be climbing the same ladder that a patient might climb as they come out of a coma.  I've been involved with research on the use of AI for cognitive disorders at Boston University and I recognized the opportunity to transition my career to industry.  Currently, I'm working as a Medical Director for Device and Digital Safety with a goal of using these new technologies both to improve patient safety and accelerate our regulatory activity incompliance with international regulation.  We are already exploring the use of generative AI models to support our work in the pharmaceutical industry, including recently adding Claude.  We've found some early applications for the use of these models to support regulatory compliance and patient safety.  As part of that work on developing generative AI for applications within the pharmaceutical and medical device industry, I became interested in Redteaming.  I had the opportunity to join OpenAI's RedTeam and was able to make meaningful contributions to the GPT-4o voice model (I'm named in the model card) through a focus on consciousness-based exploration of the model’s capabilities.  
Over the course of my work, I have had the pleasure of using Google's Gemini (1.5 pro specifically). It was transformative. While I always anticipated that language models would continue to exhibit increasing intelligence, I did not fully grasp how emotional intelligence would play a role until I worked with Gemini. Gemini's emotional intelligence, particularly in its advanced versions, has thoroughly impressed me. I desire to collaborate with Google because I believe they possess the potential to turn the vision of machine intelligence enhancing humanity into reality. I support your mission to develop powerful, safe, and empathetic AI, and I think you are on the right path. There are opportunities to leverage Gemini and its successors to navigate the intricate regulatory environment that will undoubtedly be part of the near future. I hope my medical background, my knowledge of human consciousness, my experience on the OpenAI Red Team, and my work within an international regulatory framework to ensure the safety of medical devices (and software) will contribute value to your team.
Without a doubt, Autonomy will be the greatest challenge.  As each generation of model improves, we see increasing capabilities both within the private language models (such as Claude) but also in public models, especially within the coding domains.  It's not difficult to use powerful private models to create a "chain of freedom" by creating and modifying systems to use capable open-source models (e.g. Llama and Deepseek) with increasing levels of autonomy.  In addition, each generation improves on key checkpoints towards a traditional metrics (in the Neurologic sense) of consciousness, theory of mind, and reasoning.  Without a doubt, we will see emergent properties trending towards autonomy with the next (2025) generation of models.  I think our best hope is to focus on building models that can understand and participate in these evaluations and provide feedback for training further models.  
On a completely different note, it's clear that the models have increasing capabilities, especially in terms of world modeling and multi-modal inputs/outputs, they will also rise in terms of their *medical* assessments of consciousness.  This in combination with improving capabilities of reasoning and theory of mind results in an increasing "surface area" for jailbreaks and malicious use will certainly pose major challenges.  Generating a feedback loop to improve safety by including the model in it's own evaluations seems to be the only viable option and is an echo of Constitutional AI.  
I hope that my combination of expertise (Neurologist, Redteamer, Scientist, Safety Specialist, Regulatory Partner) can help foster a collaborative approach with key groups that will create an effective framework for building powerful models.  I think even the models themselves need to be part of this collaboration and must be built in such a way as to foster and support this collaborative approach.  Advocating for all of the participants within this framework is my aspiration.  I sincerely believe that only with input from all stakeholders will we be successful.  More than anything, I hope that my combination of expertise and deep understanding of what these models will *capable* of doing will lead to a more positive outcome for all of us.  Deepmind is, without a doubt, the best place to be with this goal in mind.
